item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Looking through the discussion up thread I noticed a lot of comparison between this piece and SCP-1986. To me, both anomalies come across as two sides of the same coin in that each is a glimpse into the media of a different direction, with the media in question changing between each piece. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-1763 is a Class-4 (reclassified from Class-3 following incident 1763-64) interdimensional portal located in the basement of ████ West 38th Street, New York City, NY.1 At the time SCP-1763 was discovered, 76 folding chairs had been set up facing SCP-1763, in a formation typical of a proscenium space. While there is no box office, a metal bowl is placed on a small table at the entrance to the seating area with the label "donations". Any objects placed in the bowl will disappear at exactly one hour following the end of any performance incident. One hour before a performance incident, 40 to 70 programs will appear beside the bowl, detailing the performance that will take place along with a brief summary of the social context of the performance and an overlook of the organization performing the piece. Throughout Foundation containment of SCP-1763, only one organization has performed; however, several programs indicate that previous "companies" have made use of the venue.

To this:

SCP-1763 is a Class-4 (reclassified from Class-3 following incident 1763-64) interdimensional portal2 located in the basement of ████ West 38th Street, New York City, NY.3 At the time SCP-1763 was discovered, 76 folding chairs had been set up facing SCP-1763, in a formation typical of a proscenium space. While there is no box office, a metal bowl is placed on a small table at the entrance to the seating area with the label "donations". Any objects placed in the bowl will disappear at exactly one hour following the end of any performance incident. One hour before a performance incident, 40 to 70 programs will appear beside the bowl, detailing the performance that will take place along with a brief summary of the social context of the performance and an overlook of the organization performing the piece. Throughout Foundation containment of SCP-1763, only one organization has performed; however, several programs indicate that previous "companies" have made use of the venue.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Independent shows not necessarily associated with Broadway Theater are common in this area of New York City.
2. Similar to that observed in SCP-1986.
3. Independent shows not necessarily associated with Broadway Theater are common in this area of New York City.

item.title: Re: Image problems
item.content:encoded: He uses Reddit frequently under the username Corbutte. You could send him a PM.

item.title: Re: Image problems
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree, and have taken down the image (really just commented out the image code, but same result).

If anyone knows how to get hold of Salman Corbette to let him know about the image issue, that would be lovely.

item.title: Image problems
item.content:encoded: I've identified the source of the image (three grim reapers sitting in chairs and waiting). It comes from a 2006 PS2 ad (http://www.coloribus.com/adsarchive/prints/sony-playstation-2-death-waiting-8553155 ) , and as such is the property of Sony Corporation. I strongly recommend it be removed from the article immediately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just made me giggle like an idiot at work. Thanks for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Performance Incident #: 36
Sponsor: Valve

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-37 is just another member of the theater troupe. As for why it covers things in pink slime: look, have you seen -37 ? If you had, you'd know why.

Hats are the system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Man, I'm dense. Ba dum chiiiing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not the kind of "royalty" I meant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What royalties? It's public domain. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They can't afford to pay the performance royalties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could upvote these two comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you've hung out enough around off-off-Broadway and local theatre sorts, beyond the random vomiting, screaming, and a guy being a Neanderthal, this is a semi-accurate depiction of off-off-Broadway theaters.

(using 'off-off broadway' makes me want want to get someone to punch me in the throat)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… what happens if SCP-1763-A stages a performance of SCP-701?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a little bit of experience, but it seems you have much more than I, so I'll take your suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it related to Foundation Space Theatre?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Lighting instruments" or "Lighting units" can refer to multiple units. Either one of those phrases can refer to all of the instruments hung for a show. "Lighting equipment" could refer to everything that's in the air lighting-wise (cables, scrollers, pattern rotaters, and other stuff that aren't lights too), but yeah. It's kind of a picky terminology change. I doubt it would even register for most people reading the article and totally don't blame you for keeping it the way it is.

(Also, I'm sorry if I'm telling you stuff you already know. I assume I'm starting from scratch when I'm talking technical theater with people because it's kind of an obscure subject unless you work in it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I chose "lighting mechanism" because I thought it might be a bit overboard to imply a specific lighting instrument in a dimension where they apparently don't need any lighting equipment at all. You are completely correct about the proscenium arch bit, however, I'll change that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't have to capitalize "Proscenium Arch" and really, you can just say that's it's a proscenium space. That gets the point across that's not, say, a blackbox or theater in the round or some other kind of space that's not proscenium. And, if I were being really picky I would change "lighting mechanisms" in the fourth footnote to something like "lighting units" or "lighting instruments." Mechanism isn't really a term that's used for stage lighting equipment, unless it's a regional difference that I'm not familiar with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor quibble: How can one say that the director is incorporeal? Maybe he just is a set of mechanical hands.

Doesn't matter, liked it anyway. This give me the impression of a strictly limited view of a very strange universe, and we can't know precisely how strange it is because it's filtered through the lense of off-broadway productions; which are often sufficiently strange when the cast is fully human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've noticed a lot of people comparing this to SCP-1986 (which is fine), saying they prefer 1986's logs. I suppose this makes sense, because while a large number of the membership probably reads literature quite regularly, very few, I would guess, attend the theatre on a regular basis. Hell, even I don't go out and watch many of the more out-there performances. That being said, I'll take over from drunk Sal here and look into shortening the description (not sure how exactly I'm going to do this, but I'll spend some time thinking it over) and perhaps increasing those logs (or making a supplementary page for more).

I will, however, let you in on a bit of an Easter Egg drunk Sal seems to have included. The title of this SCP is "Found Space Theatre". Found Space theatre specifically refers to an area that was previously used for something else but was abandoned and transformed into a theatre (commonly this involves taking some kind of abandoned factory or industrial space and turning it into a small theatre space). I'll let you folks think about that one.

- Hungover Sal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed, I cried, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but I feel like SCP-1986 did this kind of thing better. I just didn't like the log of this one as much. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like how many SCP-1763-A-##s there are, it's confusing. I do like this, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's probably a reason it's redacted. And most building locations don't provide address numbers on this site, anyway

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: new yorker here. there's no '38th st' in manhattan. you have to specify whether it's west 38th or east 38th. (if that's what's blanked out, then you still need to specify an address number.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We are hats". Yes.
+1
Just a by the way, is there any significance that the director of that play's name is a palindrome?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm… I wonder what incident 1763-64 was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, Anaxagoras, for putting into words exactly what was bugging me about this article.

That said, the idea of a theatre director made out of a pair of giant mechanical arms is an image I really, really like. It's a shame I can't find the rest of the article compelling enough to upvote for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the dangling modifier an intended part of #31's description?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm actually not a fan of this. The classification is a bit confusing, and the article goes on too long before getting into the performance log, which is the real meat of the article anyhow. Once you get to those, it seems like a knock-off of SCP-1986, but with plays instead of books. Just doesn't do it for me, Sal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weird, but it really doesn't do anything for me. It doesn't really join together into a cohesive piece. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much like Performance Incident #31, this is 'oddly compelling'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"While mentions of Godot remained unchanged, "God" was replaced with "Dog" throughout the production."
Heh. That's my favorite line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of the prose part, but the Addendums saved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is SCP-1763-A-37, and why does it cover things in pink slime?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I changed my mind. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And might I say, you really surprised me with what you turned this into. I would have done something obvious, but you really took it to a higher level. Good work on the good concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sal, you have a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear sober Sal:

I hope i haven't posted this against your better judgement. I think this SCP was ripe for posting, and after some revision in the chat I have determined it is ready.

- Drunk Sal

PS I have already broken your trust by posting this as the SCP title as opposed to "SCP-1763". I have since rectified this, but do not let me do this again.

PPS I have also forgotten to give image credit to Wilt, who inspired this thing by giving me said image. Once again, I urge you to prevent me from doing this again.
